title: Use the logarithm change of base rule
description: Find the logarithmic variable expression that is equivalent to a given logarithmic expression.
topic_path: Change of base formula for logarithms
Use the logarithm change of base rule

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is equivalent to $\log(a)\cdot \log_{a}(5)$ ?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Notice that the argument of the first logarithm is the same as the base of the second logarithm.

>$\log(\blueD a)\cdot \log_{\blueD a}(5)$

If we apply the change of base rule, we will be able to cancel common factors. [[☃ explanation 1]]

Since the base on the first logarithm is $10$, let's change the base of $\log_a(5)$ to $10$.  [[☃ explanation 1]]

>$\begin{align}\log( a)\cdot \log_{a}(5)&=\greenD{\log( a)}\cdot \dfrac{{\log(5)}}{\greenD{\log(a)}}\\\\
\end{align}$

Let's cancel the common factors:

>$\begin{align}\phantom{\dfrac{\blueD{\log(4)}}{\log(3)}\cdot \dfrac{{\log(27)}}{\blueD{\log(4)}}}&=\cancel{\greenD{\log( a)}}\cdot \dfrac{{\log(5)}}{\cancel{\greenD{\log(a)}}}\\\\
&=\log(5)
\end{align}$
In conclusion, $\log(a)\cdot \log_{a}(5)=\log(5)$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is equivalent to $\log_{3}(a)\cdot \log(3)$ ?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Notice that the base of the first logarithm is the same as the argument of the second logarithm.

>$\log_{\blueD 3}(a)\cdot \log(\blueD 3)$

If we apply the change of base rule, we will be able to cancel common factors. [[☃ explanation 1]]

Since the base on the second logarithm is $10$, let's change the base of $\log_3(a)$ to $10$.   [[☃ explanation 1]]

>$\begin{align}\log_3( a)\cdot \log(3)&=\dfrac{\log( a)}{\greenD{\log(3)}}\cdot \greenD{\log(3)}\\\\
\end{align}$

Let's cancel the common factors:

>$\begin{align}\phantom{\dfrac{\blueD{\log(4)}}{\log(3)}\cdot \dfrac{{\log(27)}}{\blueD{\log(4)}}}&=\dfrac{\log( a)}{\cancel{\greenD{\log(3)}}}\cdot \cancel{\greenD{\log(3)}}\\\\
&=\log(a)
\end{align}$
In conclusion, $\log_{3}(a)\cdot \log(3)=\log(a)$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is equivalent to $\log_2(c)\cdot \log_{c}(2)$ ?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Notice that the argument of the first logarithm is the same as the base of the second logarithm and that the base of the first logarithm is the same as the argument of the second logarithm.  

>$\log_{\maroonD 2}(\blueD c)\cdot \log_{\blueD c}(\maroonD 2)$

If we apply the change of base rule, we will be able to cancel common factors. [[☃ explanation 1]]

For simplicity, let's change the base of both log expressions to $10$.  

>$\begin{align}\log_{ 2}(c)\cdot \log_{ c}( 2)&=\dfrac{\greenD {\log(c)}}{\purpleD{\log(2)}}\cdot \dfrac{\purpleD{\log(2)}}{\greenD{\log(c)}}\\\\
\end{align}$

Let's cancel the common factors:

>$\begin{align}\phantom{\dfrac{\blueD{\log(4)}}{\log(3)}\cdot \dfrac{{\log(27)}}{\blueD{\log(4)}}}&=\dfrac{\cancel{\greenD {\log(c)}}}{\cancel{\purpleD{\log(2)}}}\cdot \dfrac{\cancel{\purpleD{\log(2)}}}{\cancel{\greenD{\log(c)}}}\\\\
&=1
\end{align}$
In conclusion, $\log_2(c)\cdot \log_{c}(2)=1$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is equivalent to $\log_7(a)\cdot \log_{b}(7)$ ?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Notice that the base of the first logarithm is the same as the argument of the second logarithm.

>$\log_{\blueD7}(a)\cdot \log_{b}(\blueD7)$

If we apply the change of base rule, we will be able to cancel common factors. [[☃ explanation 1]]

For simplicity, let's change the base of both log expressions to $10$.   

>$\begin{align}\log_{7}(a)\cdot \log_{b}(7)&=\dfrac{{\log( a)}}{\greenD{\log(7)}}\cdot \dfrac{{\greenD{\log(7)}}}{{\log(b)}}\\\\
\end{align}$

Let's cancel the common factors:

>$\begin{align}\phantom{\dfrac{\blueD{\log(4)}}{\log(3)}\cdot \dfrac{{\log(27)}}{\blueD{\log(4)}}}&=\dfrac{{\log( a)}}{\cancel{\greenD{\log(7)}}}\cdot \dfrac{{\cancel{\greenD{\log(7)}}}}{{\log(b)}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\log(a)}{\log(b)}
\end{align}$

Applying the change of base rule again (in the other direction), we get:

>$\begin{align}\phantom{\dfrac{\blueD{\log(4)}}{\log(3)}\cdot \dfrac{{\log(27)}}{\blueD{\log(4)}}}&=\log_b(a)\\\\
\end{align}$
In conclusion, $\log_7(a)\cdot \log_{b}(7)=\log_b(a)$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is equivalent to $\dfrac{1}{\log_3(m)}$ ?**


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The given expression only has one logarithm.  To find an equivalent expression, however, we can try to change the base of the logarithmic part. 



For simplicity, let's change the base of the given log expression to $10$.  [[☃ explanation 1]]

If we apply the change of base rule, we get the following:  

>$\begin{align}\dfrac{1}{\log_3(m)}&=1 \div \log_3(m) \\\\
&=1 \div \left( \dfrac{\log(m)}{\log(3)}\right)&\text{Change of base rule}\\\\
&=1\cdot \left( \dfrac{\log(3)}{\log(m)}\right)&\div \text{ is} \times \text{by the reciprocal}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\log(3)}{\log(m)}
\end{align}$

Applying the change of base rule again (in the other direction), we get:

>$\begin{align}\phantom{\dfrac{1}{\log_3(m)}}&=\log_m(3)\\\\
\end{align}$
In conclusion, $\dfrac{1}{\log_3(m)}=\log_m(3)$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is equivalent to $\dfrac{1}{\log_b(4)}$ ?**


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The given expression only has one logarithm.  To find an equivalent expression, however, we can try to change the base of the logarithmic part. 



For simplicity, let's change the base of the given expression to $10$.  [[☃ explanation 1]]

If we apply the change of base rule, we get the following:  

>$\begin{align}\dfrac{1}{\log_b(4)}&=1 \div \log_b(4) \\\\
&=1 \div \left( \dfrac{\log(4)}{\log(b)}\right)&\text{Change of base rule}\\\\
&=1\cdot \left( \dfrac{\log(b)}{\log(4)}\right)&\div \text{ is} \times \text{by the reciprocal}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\log(b)}{\log(4)}
\end{align}$

Applying the change of base rule again (in the other direction), we get:

>$\begin{align}\phantom{\dfrac{1}{\log_b(4)}}&=\log_4(b)\\\\
\end{align}$
In conclusion, $\dfrac{1}{\log_b(4)}=\log_4(b)$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is equivalent to $\dfrac{\log_b(8)}{\log_b(2)}$ ?**


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Notice that the base of the logarithm in the numerator is the same as the base of the logarithm in the denominator.

>$\dfrac{\log_{\blueD b}(8)}{\log_{\blueD b}(2)}$

If we apply the change of base rule, we will be able to cancel common factors. [[☃ explanation 2]]

For simplicity, let's change the base of both logarithms to $10$.  

>$\begin{align}\dfrac{\log_{ b}(8)}{\log_{ b}(2)}&=\log_b(8)\div \log_b(2)\\\\
&=\dfrac{{\log( 8)}}{{\log(b)}}\div \dfrac{{{\log(2)}}}{{\log(b)}}\\\\
\end{align}$

Division is multiplication by the reciprocal, and so this becomes:

>$\begin{align}\phantom{\dfrac{\log(3)}{\log_n(t)}}&=\dfrac{{\log( 8)}}{{\greenD{\log(b)}}}\cdot \dfrac{{{\greenD{\log(b)}}}}{{\log(2)}}\\\\
\end{align}$

Let's cancel common factors:

>$\begin{align}\phantom{\dfrac{\log(3)}{\log_n(3)}}&=\dfrac{{\log( 8)}}{{\cancel{\greenD{\log(b)}}}}\cdot \dfrac{{{\cancel{\greenD{\log(b)}}}}}{{\log(2)}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\log(8)}{\log(2)}
\end{align}$

Applying the change of base rule again (in the other direction), we get:

>$\begin{align}\phantom{\dfrac{1}{\log_b(m)}}&=\log_2(8)\\\\
&=3
\end{align}$
In conclusion, $\dfrac{\log_b(8)}{\log_b(2)}=3$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is equivalent to $\dfrac{\log_5(m)}{\log_{15}(m)}$ ?**


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Notice that the argument of the logarithm in the numerator is the same as the argument of the logarithm in the denominator.

>$\dfrac{\log_{5}(\blueD m)}{\log_{15}(\blueD m)}$

If we apply the change of base rule, we will be able to cancel common factors. [[☃ explanation 2]]

For simplicity, let's change the base of both logarithms to $10$.  

>$\begin{align}\dfrac{\log_{ 5}(m)}{\log_{ 15}(m)}&=\log_5(m)\div \log_{15}(m)\\\\
&=\dfrac{{\log( m)}}{{\log(5)}}\div \dfrac{{{\log(m)}}}{{\log(15)}}\\\\
\end{align}$

Division is multiplication by the reciprocal, and so this becomes:

>$\begin{align}\phantom{\dfrac{\log(3)}{\log_n(t)}}&=\dfrac{{\greenD{\log( m)}}}{{{\log(5)}}}\cdot \dfrac{{{{\log(15)}}}}{{\greenD{\log(m)}}}\\\\
\end{align}$

Let's cancel common factors:

>$\begin{align}\phantom{\dfrac{\log(3)}{\log_n(3)}}&=\dfrac{{\cancel{\greenD{\log( m)}}}}{{{\log(5)}}}\cdot \dfrac{{{{\log(15)}}}}{{\cancel{\greenD{\log(m)}}}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\log(15)}{\log(5)}
\end{align}$

Applying the change of base rule again (in the other direction), we get:

>$\begin{align}\phantom{\dfrac{1}{\log_b(m)}}&=\log_5(15)\\\\
\end{align}$
In conclusion, $\dfrac{\log_5(m)}{\log_{15}(m)}=\log_5(15)$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is equivalent to $\dfrac{\log(3)}{\log_n(3)}$ ?**


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Notice that the argument of the logarithm in the numerator is the same as the argument of the logarithm in the denominator.

>$\dfrac{\log(\blueD3)}{\log_n(\blueD3)}$



If we apply the change of base rule, we will be able to cancel common factors. [[☃ explanation 2]]

Since the base of the logarithm in the numerator is $10$, let's change the base of $\log_n(3)$ to $10$.   [[☃ explanation 1]]

>$\begin{align}\dfrac{\log(3)}{\log_n(3)}&={\log( 3)}\div \log_n(3)\\\\
&=\log(3)\div\dfrac{\log(3)}{\log(n)}
\end{align}$

Division is multiplication by the reciprocal, and so this becomes:

>$\begin{align}\phantom{\dfrac{\log(3)}{\log_n(3)}}&=\greenD{\log(3)} \cdot\dfrac{\log(n)}{\greenD{\log(3)}} \\\\
\end{align}$

Let's cancel common factors:

>$\begin{align}\phantom{\dfrac{\log(3)}{\log_n(3)}}&=\cancel{\greenD{\log(3)}} \cdot\dfrac{\log(n)}{\cancel{\greenD{\log(3)}}} \\\\
&=\log(n)
\end{align}$
In conclusion, $\dfrac{\log(3)}{\log_n(3)}=\log(n)$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is equivalent to $\dfrac{\log(t)}{\log_8(t)}$ ?**


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Notice that the argument of the logarithm in the numerator is the same as the argument of the logarithm in the denominator.

>$\dfrac{\log(\blueD t)}{\log_8(\blueD t)}$



If we apply the change of base rule, we will be able to cancel common factors. [[☃ explanation 2]]

Since the base of the logarithm in the numerator is $10$, let's change the base of $\log_8(t)$ to $10$.   [[☃ explanation 1]]

>$\begin{align}\dfrac{\log(t)}{\log_8(t)}&={\log( t)}\div \log_8(t)\\\\
&=\log(t)\div\dfrac{\log(t)}{\log(8)}
\end{align}$

Division is multiplication by the reciprocal, and so this becomes:

>$\begin{align}\phantom{\dfrac{\log(3)}{\log_n(t)}}&=\greenD{\log(t)} \cdot\dfrac{\log(8)}{\greenD{\log(t)}} \\\\
\end{align}$

Let's cancel common factors:

>$\begin{align}\phantom{\dfrac{\log(3)}{\log_n(3)}}&=\cancel{\greenD{\log(t)}} \cdot\dfrac{\log(8)}{\cancel{\greenD{\log(t)}}} \\\\
&=\log(8)
\end{align}$
In conclusion, $\dfrac{\log(t)}{\log_8(t)}=\log(8)$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is equivalent to $\dfrac{\log_c(6)}{\log(6)}$ ?**


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Notice that the argument of the logarithm in the numerator is the same as the argument of the logarithm in the denominator.

>$\dfrac{\log_c(\blueD6)}{\log(\blueD6)}$



If we apply the change of base rule, we will be able to cancel common factors. [[☃ explanation 2]]

Since the base of the logarithm in the denominator is $10$, let's change the base of $\log_c(6)$ to $10$.   [[☃ explanation 1]]

>$\begin{align}\dfrac{\log_c(6)}{\log(6)}&={\log_c(6)}\div \log(6)\\\\
&=\dfrac{\log(6)}{\log(c)}\div \log(6)
\end{align}$

Division is multiplication by the reciprocal, and so this becomes:

>$\begin{align}\phantom{\dfrac{\log(3)}{\log_n(3)}}&=\dfrac{\greenD{\log(6)}}{\log(c)}\cdot \dfrac{1}{\greenD{\log(6)}} \\\\
\end{align}$

Let's cancel common factors:

>$\begin{align}\phantom{\dfrac{\log(3)}{\log_n(3)}}&=\dfrac{\cancel{\greenD{\log(6)}}}{\log(c)}\cdot \dfrac{1}{\cancel{\greenD{\log(6)}}}\\\\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{\log(c)}
\end{align}$
In conclusion, $\dfrac{\log_c(6)}{\log(6)}=\dfrac{1}{\log(c)}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is equivalent to $\dfrac{\log_9(m)}{\log(m)}$ ?**


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Notice that the argument of the logarithm in the numerator is the same as the argument of the logarithm in the denominator.

>$\dfrac{\log_9(\blueD m)}{\log(\blueD m)}$



If we apply the change of base rule, we will be able to cancel common factors. [[☃ explanation 2]]

Since the base of the logarithm in the denominator is $10$, let's change the base of $\log_9(m)$ to $10$.   [[☃ explanation 1]]

>$\begin{align}\dfrac{\log_9(m)}{\log(m)}&={\log_9(m)}\div \log(m)\\\\
&=\dfrac{\log(m)}{\log(9)}\div \log(m)
\end{align}$

Division is multiplication by the reciprocal, and so this becomes:

>$\begin{align}\phantom{\dfrac{\log(3)}{\log_n(3)}}&=\dfrac{\greenD{\log(m)}}{\log(9)}\cdot \dfrac{1}{\greenD{\log(m)}} \\\\
\end{align}$

Let's cancel common factors:

>$\begin{align}\phantom{\dfrac{\log(3)}{\log_n(3)}}&=\dfrac{\cancel{\greenD{\log(m)}}}{\log(9)}\cdot \dfrac{1}{\cancel{\greenD{\log(m)}}}\\\\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{\log(9)}
\end{align}$
In conclusion, $\dfrac{\log_9(m)}{\log(m)}=\dfrac{1}{\log(9)}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is equivalent to $\log_3(b)\cdot \log_{b}(27)$ ?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Notice that the argument of the first logarithm is the same as the base of the second logarithm.

>$\log_3(\blueD b)\cdot \log_{\blueD b}(27)$

If we apply the change of base rule, we will be able to cancel common factors. [[☃ explanation 1]]

For simplicity, let's change the base of both logarithms to $10$.  

>$\begin{align}\log_3(b)\cdot \log_{b}(27)&=\dfrac{\greenD{\log( b)}}{\log(3)}\cdot \dfrac{{\log(27)}}{\greenD{\log(b)}}\\\\
\end{align}$

Let's cancel the common factors:

>$\begin{align}\phantom{\dfrac{\blueD{\log(4)}}{\log(3)}\cdot \dfrac{{\log(27)}}{\blueD{\log(4)}}}&=\dfrac{\cancel{\greenD{\log( b)}}}{\log(3)}\cdot \dfrac{{\log(27)}}{\cancel{\greenD{\log(b)}}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\log(27)}{\log(3)}
\end{align}$

Applying the change of base rule again (in the other direction), we get:

>$\begin{align}\phantom{\log_3(b)\cdot \log_{b}(27)}&=\log_3(27)\\\\
&=3
\end{align}$
In conclusion, $\log_3(b)\cdot \log_{b}(27)=3$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is equivalent to $\log_4(m)\cdot \log_{m}(20)$ ?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Notice that the argument of the first logarithm is the same as the base of the second logarithm.

>$\log_4(\blueD m)\cdot \log_{\blueD m}(20)$

If we apply the change of base rule, we will be able to cancel common factors. [[☃ explanation 1]]

For simplicity, let's change the base of both logarithms to $10$.  

>$\begin{align}\log_4(m)\cdot \log_{m}(20)&=\dfrac{\greenD{\log( m)}}{\log(4)}\cdot \dfrac{{\log(20)}}{\greenD{\log(m)}}\\\\
\end{align}$

Let's cancel the common factors:

>$\begin{align}\phantom{\dfrac{\blueD{\log(4)}}{\log(3)}\cdot \dfrac{{\log(27)}}{\blueD{\log(4)}}}&=\dfrac{\cancel{\greenD{\log( m)}}}{\log(4)}\cdot \dfrac{{\log(20)}}{\cancel{\greenD{\log(m)}}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\log(20)}{\log(4)}
\end{align}$

Applying the change of base rule again (in the other direction), we get:

>$\begin{align}\phantom{\log_3(b)\cdot \log_{b}(27)}&=\log_4(20)\\\\
\end{align}$
In conclusion, $\log_4(m)\cdot \log_{m}(20)=\log_4(20)$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is equivalent to $\log_a(18)\cdot \log_{3}(a)$ ?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Notice that the base of the first logarithm is the same as the argument of the second logarithm.

>$\log_{\blueD a}(18)\cdot \log_{3}(\blueD a)$

If we apply the change of base rule, we will be able to cancel common factors. [[☃ explanation 1]]

For simplicity, let's change the base of both logarithms to $10$.  

>$\begin{align}\log_a(18)\cdot \log_{3}(a)&=\dfrac{{\log( 18)}}{\greenD{\log(a)}}\cdot \dfrac{{\greenD{\log(a)}}}{{\log(3)}}\\\\
\end{align}$

Let's cancel the common factors:

>$\begin{align}\phantom{\dfrac{\blueD{\log(4)}}{\log(3)}\cdot \dfrac{{\log(27)}}{\blueD{\log(4)}}}&=\dfrac{{\log( 18)}}{\cancel{\greenD{\log(a)}}}\cdot \dfrac{{\cancel{\greenD{\log(a)}}}}{{\log(3)}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\log(18)}{\log(3)}
\end{align}$

Applying the change of base rule again (in the other direction), we get:

>$\begin{align}\phantom{\log_3(b)\cdot \log_{b}(27)}&=\log_3(18)\\\\
\end{align}$
In conclusion, $\log_a(18)\cdot \log_{3}(a)=\log_3(18)$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is equivalent to $\log_c(16)\cdot \log_{2}(c)$ ?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Notice that the base of the first logarithm is the same as the argument of the second logarithm.

>$\log_{\blueD c}(16)\cdot \log_{2}(\blueD c)$

If we apply the change of base rule, we will be able to cancel common factors. [[☃ explanation 1]]

For simplicity, let's change the base of both logarithms to $10$.  

>$\begin{align}\log_c(16)\cdot \log_{2}(c)&=\dfrac{{\log( 16)}}{\greenD{\log(c)}}\cdot \dfrac{{\greenD{\log(c)}}}{{\log(2)}}\\\\
\end{align}$

Let's cancel the common factors:

>$\begin{align}\phantom{\dfrac{\blueD{\log(4)}}{\log(3)}\cdot \dfrac{{\log(27)}}{\blueD{\log(4)}}}&=\dfrac{{\log( 16)}}{\cancel{\greenD{\log(c)}}}\cdot \dfrac{{\cancel{\greenD{\log(c)}}}}{{\log(2)}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\log(16)}{\log(2)}
\end{align}$

Applying the change of base rule again (in the other direction), we get:

>$\begin{align}\phantom{\log_3(b)\cdot \log_{b}(27)}&=\log_2(16)\\\\
&=4
\end{align}$
In conclusion, $\log_c(16)\cdot \log_{2}(c)=4$.
